title: DSA
description: Data structures and algorithms — complexity, arrays, stacks, trees, and graphs.

lessons.title: DSA Introduction
lessons.subtitle: Why structures and algorithms matter
lessons.theory:
Data structures organize data; algorithms are step-by-step procedures that use those structures to solve problems.

Choosing the right structure affects time and memory. A hash map offers fast lookups; a sorted array enables binary search.

Interview prep and production systems both rely on understanding trade-offs, not memorizing every trick.

You will use Big O notation to compare growth rates as input size increases.

definition.label: Definition
definition.text: DSA is the study of how to store data efficiently and design procedures that run in acceptable time and space.

codeExample.label: Simple timing idea

quiz.question: What does DSA stand for?
quiz.explanation: DSA is the pairing of data structures with algorithms that process them.

lessons.title: Big O Basics
lessons.subtitle: Time and space complexity
lessons.theory:
Big O describes upper-bound growth as n grows. O(1) is constant time; O(n) grows linearly; O(n²) grows with nested loops over n.

Drop constants and lower terms: O(2n + 5) is still O(n). Focus on dominant behavior for large inputs.

Space complexity counts extra memory beyond the input. An in-place algorithm might be O(1) extra space.

Worst-case analysis is common unless the problem specifies average case.

definition.label: Definition
definition.text: Big O notation expresses how resource usage scales with input size n, ignoring constant factors.

keyPoints: O(1), O(log n), O(n), O(n log n), O(n²) are common
Binary search is O(log n) on sorted data
Space and time are analyzed separately

codeExample.label: O(n²) pattern

quiz.question: What is the complexity of scanning an array once?
quiz.explanation: A single loop over n elements is linear: O(n).

lessons.title: Arrays and Strings
lessons.subtitle: Indexing, two pointers, and sliding window
lessons.theory:
Arrays offer O(1) random access by index but O(n) insertion in the middle of a contiguous array.

Many problems use two pointers from both ends or a sliding window to avoid redundant scans.

Strings are often treated as character arrays. Immutability in some languages means building new strings costs extra.

Prefix sums precompute cumulative totals for fast range queries.

definition.label: Definition
definition.text: An array is a contiguous sequence of elements addressed by integer indices, usually with O(1) access time.

codeExample.label: Two sum sorted (concept)

quiz.question: Random access by index in a static array is typically?
quiz.explanation: Computing base + index * element size gives constant-time access.

lessons.title: Stacks and Queues
lessons.subtitle: LIFO, FIFO, and when to use them
lessons.theory:
A stack supports push and pop at one end — Last In, First Out (LIFO). Use it for parsing, undo, and DFS.

A queue supports enqueue at the back and dequeue at the front — First In, First Out (FIFO). BFS on graphs uses a queue.

Both can be implemented with arrays or linked lists. Amortized array resizing is common in practice.

Deque (double-ended queue) allows efficient operations at both ends.

definition.label: Definition
definition.text: A stack removes the most recently added item first; a queue removes the oldest waiting item first.

keyPoints: Queue: enqueue/dequeue O(1) with proper design

codeExample.label: Stack with array
codeExample.code:
const stack = []
stack.push(1)
stack.push(2)
const top = stack.pop() // 2

quiz.question: Which structure is LIFO?
quiz.explanation: Stacks are Last In, First Out.

lessons.title: Trees and Graphs
lessons.subtitle: Hierarchies, traversals, and search
lessons.theory:
A tree is a connected acyclic graph with a root. Binary trees have at most two children per node.

Traversals visit nodes in orders: preorder, inorder, postorder, or level-order (BFS).

Binary search trees keep left smaller and right larger, enabling O(log n) search when balanced.

Graphs add cycles and arbitrary connections. Represent with adjacency lists or matrices; run BFS/DFS for reachability.

definition.label: Definition
definition.text: A tree is a hierarchical structure of nodes and edges without cycles; a graph generalizes connections between entities.

keyPoints: BFS uses a queue; DFS uses stack/recursion
Adjacency list is space-efficient for sparse graphs

codeExample.label: BFS skeleton

quiz.question: Which traversal uses a queue on a tree?
quiz.explanation: Level-order traversal processes nodes breadth-first with a queue.
